exemplar.title: How does the amount of current running through a solenoid with an iron core affect the magnetic flux density through it?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does the amount of current running through a solenoid with an iron core affect the magnetic flux density through it?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data processing (averaging the 30 001-point time series, baseline correction, linear regression) is performed correctly and consistently.
Tables and graphs are clearly labeled with units and error bars; sample calculations illustrate each processing step.
Uncertainties from instruments are propagated through mean calculations and linearâfit parameters, including maximum/minimumâslope analysis, showing appropriate consideration of uncertainties.
weaknesses: Uncertainty propagation for the derived quantity k (relative permeability) and intercept uncertainty is omitted.
Inconsistent unit formatting and minor stylistic issues reduce the overall precision of communication.
Graphs lack a legend or clear definitions for different line styles (best-fit vs. max/min slopes) and errorâbar captions, slightly hindering clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The data analysis is both clear and precise, with wellâexecuted processing and appropriate uncertainty treatment, placing it firmly in the 5â6 band. Small gapsâchiefly omission of kâs uncertainty and minor communication inconsistenciesâprevent a perfect 6, so a score of 5 is justified.

strengths: Research question framed within a specific, real-world context linking solenoid behavior to transformers, valves, and rail guns.
Methodology is described in sufficient detailâequipment models with uncertainties, sampling rate, trial count, baseline correctionâenabling reproducibility.

Methodological considerations (controls for probe orientation, averaging sinusoidal data, baseline subtraction) are explained, demonstrating a clear understanding of collecting relevant and sufficient data.
weaknesses: No circuit schematic to clarify current direction and component connections in the experimental setup.
Numerical design targets (e.g., expected flux density at given current) and assumptions behind key equations (coil spacing, ideal solenoid geometry) are not specified.
Tolerance values for series resistors and control description for coil dimensions are omitted, leaving small ambiguities in uncertainty analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student situates the research question within a well-defined, appropriate context and provides detailed equipment lists, procedural steps, and theoretical rationale, satisfying the 5â6 band. Minor omissions (circuit diagram, numerical targets, resistor tolerances) prevent a top-band score of 6, so a holistic score of 5 accurately reflects the high-quality yet slightly incomplete research design.

strengths: Conclusion is justified and fully consistent with the analysis, restating the proportional relationship and noting that the zero intercept lies within uncertainty.
Relevant comparison to accepted scientific context (AmpÃ¨reâs law, literature on finite-length solenoids) explains the anomalous permeability factor.
The student links experimental slope and uncertainty to theoretical expectations, demonstrating strong conceptual synthesis.
weaknesses: Quantitative depth is limited; the theoretical range of k for an iron-core solenoid is not provided.
Key quantitative results (slope value and uncertainty) are not explicitly summarized in the conclusion to reinforce findings.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is well supported by the data and includes a justified comparison with scientific theory, meriting a 5. It falls short of the 6âband only due to missing deeper quantitative context and a succinct summary of critical numerical outcomes.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identifiedâunstable power supply, sensor precision limits, non-ideal solenoid geometry, probe positioningâwith clear qualitative impact assessments.

Realistic and relevant improvements are described (better supply stability, higher-precision magnetometer, longer/tighter solenoid, central field measurements), each directly addressing a listed limitation.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each methodological weakness on overall uncertainty is not quantified.
Suggested improvements are described but not explained in terms of how they would quantitatively reduce uncertainties.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation outlines specific limitations and proposes feasible improvements, aligning with the top of the 3â4 band. However, without quantitative estimates of impact or improvement, it does not reach the explanatory depth required for a 5â6 band, warranting a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student frames the research question after a concise, real-world introduction that explains why understanding the currentâmagnetic-flux relationship in solenoids matters for transformers, valves and rail guns.  Background theory (AmpÃ¨reâs law, equations 2â3) and practical details (iron-core solenoid, laboratory setting) position the investigation in a **specific and appropriate** context rather than a generic one.  Minor gapsâe.g. limited reference to numerical design targetsâprevent the fullest sophistication, but the description clearly exceeds a broad outline.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted; propagation is shown for means, baseline subtraction and linear-fit parameters (âm, âb).  Error bars appear on the graph, and maximum/minimum-slope analysis gives a justified range.  While the uncertainty in k is not propagated, overall treatment is **appropriate and largely complete**, warranting the upper band, score 5.

Variables are organised in two tables, each linked to theoretical justification (Âµ, n, Earthâs field).  Control strategies (same solenoid, probe orientation) are explained; sensor precision and sampling arguments are provided; safety and ethical issues addressed.  The rationale for averaging sinusoidal data and baseline correction shows an **explained understanding** of collecting sufficient, relevant data.  A few practical tolerances (wire diameter, ambient temperature) are not addressed, so a conservative 5 is awarded.

Four specific weaknesses are listed (unstable supply, imprecise magnetometer, non-ideal solenoid, probe position) with clear descriptions of how each could bias results.  The discussion mentions relative significance qualitatively (e.g. random errors average out, non-ideal solenoid is main cause) but stops short of quantitatively evaluating impact, placing it firmly in the 3â4 band; the completeness merits the top of that band (4).

The equipment list gives model names and instrumental uncertainties; the procedure states sampling rate (1000âHz), duration (30 s), number of trials (5Ã8), baseline subtraction and the exact positioning of probes.  These details allow another student to replicate the data-collection process with **no substantive ambiguities**.  The only small omission is the exact resistance value added in series, hence a 5 not 6.

Data processing involves averaging 30001-point time series, baseline correction, linear regression and determination of slope uncertainty.  Calculations are correct and internally consistent with the presented data; derived quantities (k) are logically obtained.  Very small discrepancies (rounding, omission of intercept uncertainty in k) prevent a perfect 6, so a solid 5 is assigned.

Improvements correspond directly to each limitation (better power supply, higher-precision magnetometer, longer/tighter solenoid, central field measurement) and are feasible.  Explanations of how each fix would enhance accuracy are present but not deeply elaborated (e.g. no quantitative estimate of improvement), so they are **described rather than fully explained**, earning a strong 4/6.

Raw data, processed averages and uncertainty ranges are presented in neatly formatted tables (Tables 3â4, Appendix A).  Graphs include labelled axes with units, error bars and best-fit lines.  Sample calculations illustrate each processing step.  Minor stylistic inconsistencies (e.g. unit formatting) keep the work just short of perfect precision, so 5/6 is appropriate.

The student compares findings to AmpÃ¨reâs law (Eq. 3) and calculates an unexpected k = 0.66, then explains the deviation using finite-length and coil spacing arguments, citing literature.  This is a **relevant, justified comparison with accepted scientific context**.  Some quantitative depth (e.g. theoretical k range for iron) is missing, so 5 not 6.

The conclusion restates that magnetic flux density is proportional to current and explicitly cites the experimental slope, uncertainty and intercept range that encloses zero, making it **fully consistent with the analysis**.  It also recognises the caveat of non-ideal solenoids, but minor quantitative depth is lacking; hence 5/6.

annotations.comment: Consider adding a circuit schematic indicating current direction and component connections to clarify the experimental arrangement.
It would be helpful to show how uncertainties were propagated through the baseline subtraction for each adjusted value in Table 4.
The sample calculation of the arithmetic mean is presented step by step, enhancing transparency of data processing.
The student situates the research question within a specific real-world context, linking solenoid applications to transformers and rail guns, which strengthens the investigationâs relevance.
Consider specifying numerical design targets (e.g., expected flux density at a given current) to give the research question sharper operational clarity.
Equation (3) would benefit from an explicit statement of assumptions regarding coil spacing and ideal solenoid geometry to clarify its applicability.
The explanation distinguishing Î¼ and Î¼â clarifies the role of relative permeability in the iron-core solenoid, demonstrating strong theoretical understanding.
Table 1 clearly defines dependent and independent variables with measurement methods and relevant notes, supporting reproducibility of the data collection.
The method of control for coil dimensions is left blank in Table 2. Include a description of how coil number and spacing are standardized across trials.
The equipment list presents specific models with stated uncertainties (e.g., Â±0.004 mT, Â±0.00031 A), enhancing the precision and reproducibility of measurements.
Include tolerance values for the 5.6 Î© and 10 Î© resistors to fully characterize their impact on current uncertainty.
Procedural steps include sampling rate, duration, trial count, and baseline subtraction, providing clear reproducibility of the method.
Graph 1 would be strengthened by fitting a sine function and reporting the goodness-of-fit (e.g., RÂ² value) to support the averaging approach.
Specify unit formatting consistently (e.g., include a space before units) to improve clarity and precision in data communication.
Table 3 communicates averaged currents and flux densities with clear uncertainty notation, demonstrating precise data reporting.
Propagating the uncertainty of k (relative permeability) through the linear-fit parameters would offer a more comprehensive uncertainty analysis.
The impact of each methodological limitation is described qualitatively but would benefit from quantifying their relative contributions to the overall uncertainty.
Citation formatting varies across entries (e.g., inconsistent date and URL styles); adopting a consistent referencing style will enhance academic rigor.
On Graph 2, include definitions of error bars in the caption to clarify which uncertainties are being represented.
Add a legend or distinct line styles for the best-fit, maximum-slope, and minimum-slope lines to avoid ambiguity in Graph 3.
The description of maximum and minimum slope determination demonstrates appropriate consideration of uncertainties and is well explained.
The analysis explicitly links experimental findings to AmpÃ¨reâs law and justifies that zero intercept lies within the uncertainty range, reinforcing theoretical consistency.
The student critically evaluates the anomalous value of k and identifies specific non-ideal factors (length, coil spacing, measurement location) affecting permeability.
Consider summarizing key quantitative results (e.g., slope value and uncertainty) directly in the conclusion to reinforce the experimental outcome.
Provide estimates of how each proposed improvement (e.g., longer solenoid, precise sensor) could quantitatively reduce identified uncertainties.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.

userSubmission.researchQuestion: I will investigate the relationship between the enclosed current in a solenoid (27.1mA, 40.2mA, 53.7mA, 80.4mA, 106.7mA), and the magnetic field strength produced by that solenoid (mT). The solenoid will be consistent each trial, consisting of an insulated copper wire wrapped 60 times around an iron core that has a radius of 1cm and length of 15cm.
